features.title: Tenant-aware ingestion: scanned content must land in the tenant that registered the source
acceptance: Ingestion indexes into the tenant that OWNS the source, not DEFAULT_TENANT_ID: the tenant travels source -> queue job -> worker -> DocumentSink, and createIndexingDocumentSink/createGraphExtractionSink index through the tenant-scoped views (ADR-0033), replacing today's unconditional default-tenant write
A scan under tenant A makes the content searchable/compilable AS TENANT A, and invisible to tenant B (cross-tenant e2e over a real runtime, mirroring the F-048 full-stack suite)

A scan can no longer report success while indexing nothing the caller can see: the scan summary (or a follow-up status) reflects what was actually indexed FOR THAT TENANT — today `added: 3` is returned while the tenant sees zero results
The F-048 full-stack suite is switched off the default tenant onto a real tenant (its TENANT constant + the comment pointing here are removed); workspace gates green incl. e2e-full
features.notes: FOUND BY F-048 (2026-07-16), the first test to drive a real multi-tenant deployment end-to-end. `createIndexingDocumentSink` (packages/config/src/sources/ingestion-sink.ts) calls `indexer.indexDocument({...})` with NO tenantId, so corpus-indexer defaults it to DEFAULT_TENANT_ID; its own comment records this as the 'F-038 boundary'. Consequence: in ANY token/OIDC (multi-tenant) deployment, registering + scanning a source reports success (`added: N`) while the content is indexed into `default` and is INVISIBLE to the tenant that registered it — search/compile return nothing and the graph is empty for that tenant. Verified against a real Local-profile server: tenant `acme` -> search [] / graph empty / compile 0 sections; the identical run under `default` -> 3 ranked hits, 6 graph nodes, a 533-token package. No existing gate catches it because api/mcp e2e run in the zero-auth default tenant. F-048's suite therefore pins the deployment to the default tenant (single-tenant local, the shipped ADR-0003 shape) and documents why at the TENANT constant. Note the graph-extraction sink shares the flaw. Likely needs an ADR: the tenant must be carried on the queue job (the sink is constructed once per runtime, not per tenant).

features.title: MCP stdio credential channel: token/OIDC deployments cannot authenticate an agent over stdio
acceptance: An agent connecting over stdio to a token- (or OIDC-) mode deployment can present a credential and be authenticated into the right principal/tenant; the mechanism is documented for operators (an agent client launches tessera-mcp with a config it controls)

The credential never lands in a place a process listing or a shell history exposes casually; the choice (env var vs file vs handshake) is recorded with its threat model — an ADR if it deviates from a documented default
Gateway audit (F-047) attributes stdio calls to that principal; a missing/invalid credential is a clean UNAUTHORIZED, and zero-auth `none` mode is unchanged
MCP e2e covers the authenticated stdio path; the F-048 agent journey is switched onto it (its TESSERA_AUTH_MODE=none override + the note pointing here are removed)
features.notes: FOUND BY F-048 (2026-07-16). `defaultCredentialResolver` (apps/mcp/src/gateway.ts) reads the SDK `authInfo.token` or an `Authorization` header — both are populated only by an HTTP transport's auth middleware. Over **stdio** neither exists, so in token mode every tool call fails UNAUTHORIZED and there is no supported way to hand `tessera-mcp` a token; nothing in .env.example offers one. The gateway's own doc comment claims it 'works over stdio (one identity)', which is true only in zero-auth `none` mode. Today's real local shape is TESSERA_AUTH_MODE=none + TESSERA_AUTH_TENANT=<tenant>, which is what F-048's agent journey drives (documented at that override). This blocks a hosted/multi-client stdio deployment and interacts with F-055 (API-key principals). Options to weigh: a TESSERA_MCP_TOKEN env credential (simplest; note it lands in the agent client's config file), a token file path, or the `resolveCredential` seam the gateway already exposes.

features.title: Search results are labelled by content hash — ingested documents carry no human-readable label
acceptance: A search hit over ingested content carries a human-readable label/path (its source path), so the dashboard shows `src/ledger.ts` rather than a 64-char content hash — today the web falls back to `result.label ?? result.ref` and renders the hash
The label rides the existing Candidate/retrieval contract (the corpus already stores `path` + `sourceId` in fragment metadata); REST + MCP + SDK stay in parity (ADR-0036)
Covered by the F-048 full-stack suite: the human journey asserts a readable path in the search results (replacing its provenance-signal-only assertion)
features.notes: Done 2026-07-16 — DELIVERED BY F-061 (operator-approved fold; see the OQ-1 section of .harness/plans/F-061-search-depth.md). Not a contract problem: `Candidate.label` already existed (packages/retrieval/src/domain.ts:25, comment "Optional human-readable label/snippet"), fusion already carried it first-wins, the Zod schema already declared it, and the COMMITTED SDK already typed it — only keyword/semantic/temporal (the three that index ingested content) never populated it. The fix is createEnrichedRetriever reading metadata.path off the corpus fragment F-061 fetches anyway: ~3 lines, zero wire change, no ADR (it populates a field the contract already declares). Labels are ALWAYS on, so this is fixed by default for the dashboard AND for agents — the perf cost (56 tokens measured on a 10-result answer) was a deliberate, escalated decision, not a silent one. Proven end-to-end: the F-048 human journey now asserts a readable path and ZERO 64-char hashes, and the agent journey asserts `label` while KEEPING its keyword-signal proof (a prettier assertion is not a better one). Its own acceptance clause — "the F-048 suite asserts a readable label" — is satisfied. NOTE (F-061 SL-4): labelling both ref spaces makes the pre-existing duplicate-row wart VISIBLE (one file has a documentIdFor ref and a nodeIdFor ref, unmergeable by fusion) — surfaced rather than papered over, and registered as F-076.

features.title: Core Web Vitals gate for public surfaces: a trustworthy lab measurement for continuously-animated pages
acceptance: A LAB measurement of LCP / CLS / TBT for the public marketing surface that is trustworthy on pages running continuous rAF/WebGL animation, asserted against the declared targets already committed in tests/web-perf/budgets.json (marketing: lcpMs 2000, cls 0.05, tbtMs 200 — sourced from marketing-design.manifest.json budgets.webVitals)

The measurement technique is justified in the code, not just chosen: the candidates are (a) freeze/disable the art animations for the trace via prefers-reduced-motion — the site already renders designed still frames under it, so this may be both trustworthy AND the honest thing to measure, (b) drive PerformanceObserver directly through Playwright (real observed LCP/CLS, no Lighthouse model), or (c) Lighthouse with devtools throttling. Whichever is chosen, the report must name the metric honestly (TBT is INP's LAB PROXY — Lighthouse cannot measure INP, a field metric)

The gate is stable across repeated runs on the same machine (demonstrated, not assumed — a flaky perf gate is a lie) and is added to the existing web-perf gate + its CI step; the $vitalsNote in budgets.json and the scope-limit paragraph in the web-perf gate description are updated to say it is now enforced
NFR-17's Core Web Vitals clause is satisfied by a gate rather than by documentation; workspace gates green
features.notes: FOUND BY F-049 (2026-07-16), which activated the web-perf gate on bundle budgets but could NOT deliver the CWV half of its acceptance honestly. Lighthouse 13.4 was implemented against the real production build and removed again, with evidence: under its DEFAULT simulated throttling it reported a **TBT of 71,670 ms inside a ~10 s trace** (the marketing hero runs a WebGL ShaderField + a Canvas Constellation on continuous rAF loops, so the trace is wall-to-wall long tasks and the simulation model extrapolates from noise); switching to throttlingMethod:'provided' produced **TBT NaN and performance score 0**, with an unthrottled localhost LCP of 136 ms that represents nothing. Neither number is gateable, and shipping a gate on either would be gating on noise — so CWV was left declared-but-unenforced rather than faked, and NFR-17's CWV clause is NOT yet satisfied. The declared targets and the reasoning are already committed in tests/web-perf/budgets.json ($vitalsNote + the per-app vitals blocks) so this feature starts with its acceptance written. Related lesson: [[playwright-a11y-assertions-avoid-framework-overlays]] already records that these infinite art animations break screenshot capture — the same root cause, a second time, which is itself an argument for option (a).

features.title: Tenant-key the blob corpus so file bodies can be served safely (unblocks fragment reads)
acceptance: The corpus is tenant-partitioned: `putFragment`/`createBlobFragmentSource` key blobs per tenant (e.g. `{tenantId}/{ref}`) and the compiler + search enrichment read through a tenant-scoped FragmentSource; a migration moves existing unprefixed keys
A `GET /v1/fragments/:ref` (or equivalent) can then serve a file BODY without a cross-tenant IDOR: tenant A presenting tenant B ref gets 404, proven by an api e2e
The F-061 search detail Sheet renders full file bodies (today it shows only the excerpt — its stated SL-2 limit); memory bodies already render via the tenant-scoped memory route
Workspace gates green incl. e2e-full
features.notes: FOUND BY F-061 (2026-07-16). `BlobStore` (packages/storage/src/ports/blob.ts) has NO `forTenant`, and `putFragment` writes an UNPREFIXED key = the ref (packages/config/src/fragment-source.ts). Today that is safe BY CONSTRUCTION — every ref reaching the corpus came from an already-`forTenant`-scoped retriever, which is exactly why F-061 enrichment is safe — but it means ANY by-ref read endpoint would be a cross-tenant IDOR, and refs are DERIVABLE (`sha256(sourceId:path)`), not secret. That is the ADR-0050 class of defect: a surface that authenticates but does not authorize. F-061 therefore ships the detail Sheet with an excerpt for file results rather than a body, and says so (SL-2). F-071 compounds this: ingestion writes to DEFAULT_TENANT_ID, so registry-derived ownership would not even agree with what search returns — sequence F-071 first. Likely needs an ADR (key layout + migration).

features.title: One file, one ref: unify the document and graph-node ref spaces so fusion can merge them
acceptance: A single file is ONE row in a fused result set: the keyword/semantic/temporal hit and the graph/symbolic hit for the same file share a ref (or are reconciled before fusion), so their signals combine into one candidate with full attribution instead of two competing rows
Ranking stays honest: the reconciliation must not silently re-score or re-rank (the fusion tests are the regression guard and must pass unmodified)
A test proves a file matched by BOTH a keyword hit and a graph hit yields one candidate carrying both signals
Workspace gates green incl. the perf gate (fewer duplicate rows changes the token count)
features.notes: FOUND BY F-061 (2026-07-16), and MADE VISIBLE by it. The same file has two refs: the document ref `documentIdFor(sourceId, path) = sha256(sourceId:path)` (packages/ingestion/src/domain.ts) used by keyword/semantic/temporal, and the graph node id `nodeIdFor(kind, key) = sha256(kind:key)` (packages/knowledge-graph/src/domain.ts) used by graph/symbolic. `fuse()` keys on `ref` and therefore CANNOT merge them, so one file can occupy two rows of one result set with its signals split across them — which also understates the fused score of a file that genuinely matched on several signals. Before F-061 this was invisible (one row rendered a hash, the other a node label, so they looked like different things); labelling both makes the duplicate obvious. F-061 deliberately SURFACED the wart rather than papering over it: deduping post-fusion would corrupt ranks/scores, and unifying the ref spaces is a retrieval-architecture change. Needs an ADR (which space wins, or a reconciliation layer, and what it does to the fusion contract).

features.title: MCP compile bypasses the plan entitlement clamp — an agent can request any token budget
acceptance: The MCP `compile_context` and `explain` tools enforce the caller's plan cap, so an over-cap budget cannot produce an over-cap package: an mcp e2e asks for a budget above the free cap and asserts the returned package is bounded by the cap (today it is granted in full)

The silent-vs-explicit question is decided in an ADR and applied consistently: REST clamps SILENTLY today, so either MCP clamps silently too (behaviour parity) or BOTH surfaces tell the caller their budget was reduced. An agent quietly downgraded cannot tell a clamp from a thin corpus — argue it, do not default to it

One implementation, not two: the clamp is applied through the same `clampBudgetToPlan` + `effectiveEntitlements` path REST uses (two copies of an entitlement rule WILL drift — the F-060/F-061 lesson), and neither surface can be extended without the other
Workspace gates green incl. e2e and e2e-full
features.notes: FOUND BY F-062 (2026-07-17), verified against the tree, NOT folded in — deliberately, and the reasoning is the point. apps/api/src/routes/v1/compile.ts:41 clamps via clampBudgetToPlan(entitlements, budget) over `services.billing ?? createLocalBilling()`, so free-tier caps (packages/billing/src/domain.ts: free 8000 / pro 32000 / enterprise 128000) apply by default. apps/mcp/src/server.ts has ZERO references to billing or clampBudget, and toCompileRequest (:123-138) forwards `budget: args.budget` verbatim to BOTH compile_context (:206) and explain (:357). So NFR-12's per-plan cap is enforced on the surface humans use and unenforced on the surface AGENTS use — which is the population it exists to meter. Structurally this is the F-060 defect again (one surface hardened, its twin walked past) and the F-061 lesson (one rule, two implementations, inevitable drift). WHY NOT FOLDED (unlike F-060's SSE leak): F-062 does not AMPLIFY it — F-060 was about to pipe leaked data onto every page, whereas F-062 renders a claim about the REST surface that is true of the surface it describes. Golden rule 2 + the F-048 precedent (which registered F-071/072/073 rather than fixing them) therefore apply. Consequence F-062 respects: its clamp copy is scoped to the OBSERVED compile ('Capped to 8,000 — you requested 20,000'), never a claim of system-wide enforcement, which would be false while this stands. NEEDS AN ADR: silent clamp vs explicit rejection — an agent that is quietly downgraded cannot distinguish a clamp from a thin corpus, and REST has the same problem; decide it once, for both.

features.title: The shipped SQLite audit adapter never runs the shared AuditLog conformance suite
acceptance: createSqliteAuditLog runs `runAuditLogConformance` — the SAME suite the in-memory adapter runs — so the port contract is proven against the adapter that actually ships, not only against the reference one

The plumbing is decided and recorded: the port + conformance live in `@tessera/api` and the adapter in `@tessera/config`, so either the suite is exported from `@tessera/api` (a legitimate public artifact for adapter authors — the pattern @tessera/memory and @tessera/knowledge-graph already use INSIDE their packages) or the adapter moves. Do not solve it by duplicating assertions
The duplicated clamp assertion in packages/config/src/audit/sqlite-audit-log.test.ts is deleted once the suite covers it
Workspace gates green
features.notes: FOUND BY F-063 (2026-07-17) — and it is the ROOT CAUSE of a real divergence F-063 had to fix. `apps/api/src/audit/audit-log.conformance.ts` exists and is good, but `runAuditLogConformance` is called from exactly ONE place: `apps/api/src/audit/in-memory-audit-log.test.ts`. The SQLite adapter — the one every real deployment uses (ADR-0034) — has only its own 4 hand-written tests and has never run the shared suite. The predictable result: `sqlite-audit-log.ts` had `const limit = query.limit ?? 50` with NO clamp and a hardcoded default, while the in-memory adapter did `Math.min(query.limit ?? DEFAULT_AUDIT_PAGE_SIZE, MAX_AUDIT_PAGE_SIZE)`. A port contract only the REFERENCE adapter honours is not a contract — it is a suggestion, and the divergence sat undetected because nothing could see it. Not reachable over HTTP (the route schema caps `limit` at MAX_AUDIT_PAGE_SIZE), but F-063's audit-export trail walk calls the PORT directly, below that schema, which is exactly where it lived. F-063 fixed the clamp, added the missing case to the shared suite (covering in-memory), and DUPLICATED the assertion into the SQLite adapter's own test with a comment pointing here — because wiring the suite across a package boundary is a test-architecture change, not a dashboard story (golden rule 2; the F-048/F-062 precedent of registering rather than folding). Contrast with @tessera/memory and @tessera/knowledge-graph, whose sqlite adapters DO run their conformance suites — because port and adapter live in the same package there. This is the cross-package case nobody wired up.

features.title: Live activity is dropped on every route but Overview; the command palette crashes on keydown without a key
acceptance: A scan triggered FROM /sources increments the notifications bell without navigating to Overview — the exact path that fails today

useFeedIngest() is mounted once at an app-wide client boundary whose lifetime matches the bell that consumes it; the route-local call in dashboard.tsx is deleted in the same increment (a surviving second mount = duplicate feed entries)
A keydown with `key` undefined (browser autofill / password managers / IME) does not throw; an IME composition `k` does not open the palette
Regression tests exist that FAIL on the current code: (a) events emitted while off-Overview land in the notifications store, (b) keydown without `key` does not throw
apps/web gates green (typecheck/lint/format/test/build)
features.notes: REPORTED BY THE USER (2026-07-17), reported as intermittent, actually deterministic — and the two halves are unrelated defects that happened to be found together. (1) THE FEED. lib/api/events.ts is not at fault: EventsProvider correctly owns ONE app-wide EventSource and fans out to subscribers, and the socket IS live on /sources. The defect is one layer up: useFeedIngest() — the only code that pushes stream events into the useNotifications store — is called exclusively from components/dashboard.tsx:37, and Dashboard renders only at /. On /sources, where scanning is actually triggered, the handler set is EMPTY: frames arrive, parse, and are dropped. Navigating back mounts the ingest after the events have passed, so the store is empty and the feed says "No activity this session" — truthfully, from its point of view. The comment at dashboard.tsx:35 ("Mount once — the bell reads the same entries") had exactly the right intent and picked a mount point that only exists on one route: a GLOBAL consumer (the bell renders in app-header.tsx on every route, read-only) fed by a ROUTE-LOCAL producer. "Sometimes" in the report = the cases where the user happened to be on Overview while a scan completed (e.g. autoScanOnRegister). Note this is NOT the ADR-0050/F-071 tenant gap documented in routes/v1/events.ts — that one drops document.* for non-default tenants; this drops EVERYTHING for every tenant, including the tenant-correct source.scan.*. F-071 would not have fixed it. (2) THE PALETTE. command-palette.tsx:34 does event.key.toLowerCase(); KeyboardEvent.key is undefined for keydown synthesised by autofill and IME composition, and the listener is bound to document, so any such event anywhere on the page throws. Fix guards the read AND skips isComposing — an IME "k" is text, not a shortcut. Plan: .harness/plans/F-079-live-activity-and-palette-defects.md DONE 2026-07-17: FeedIngest mounted once in app/providers.tsx inside EventsProvider (route-local call deleted in the same change); isPaletteShortcut() extracted as a pure predicate guarding typeof key + isComposing. Gates green (state/typecheck 38-38/lint 22-22/format/test 36-36/build 19-19). Both regression tests PROVEN to fail without the fix: reverting FeedIngest gives "expected [] to have a length of 1" (the user empty feed exactly); reverting the guard gives the reported TypeError. feed-ingest.test.tsx also renders the REAL Providers, so deleting the mount fails the suite. No effect-link added (no shared contract changed; sole importer resolved in-change).

features.title: Overview v2: retire the greeting hero, gate onboarding on real state, bound the feed
acceptance: The greeting HeroBand is removed; ADR-0047 is superseded in part by ADR-0053, recording why an authenticated dashboard leads with state rather than a marketing band

The "Get started" card renders only while the workspace is provably empty, gated on /v1/stats sources+documents — NOT on the activity feed, which is session-only and empties on reload; an unknown workspace (loading/error) shows no onboarding rather than guessed onboarding
Recent activity scrolls internally within a bounded height instead of growing the page, and the scroll region is keyboard-reachable (axe scrollable-region-focusable, WCAG 2.1.1)
The Overview keeps an accessible h1 after the hero (which owned it) is removed
apps/web gates green; design-review + contrast gates pass across 4 themes x light/dark
features.notes: REPORTED BY THE USER (2026-07-17), items 1/2/3/5. THE SIGNAL CORRECTION IS THE LOAD-BEARING PART: the user proposed gating "Get started" on the Recent-activity feed being empty. That signal is wrong and would ship a worse bug than the one it fixes — the feed is session-only BY DESIGN (lib/store/notifications.ts is explicit: "a reload starts empty", F-065 is what persists it), so "Get started" would flicker back on every refresh for a workspace with sources connected, telling an established user to go connect a source. The honest signal for "is this workspace empty" is the same one the stat cards already read: /v1/stats (sources, documents), which is persisted and tenant-scoped. THE CHART HAS THE SAME TRAP: "activity over time" cannot come from the feed either (session-only, capped at FEED_LIMIT=50) — charting it would draw a line that resets to zero on reload, which is fabrication with extra steps and violates the no-fabricated-data rule the Overview already honours. Real persisted history DOES exist: the audit log (timestamped, tenant-scoped, date-filterable + exportable since F-063) and memory lineages — the same two sources timeline.ts already merges. Aggregate server-side (a client paging the whole audit log to draw a sparkline is not enterprise-grade). REMOVING THE HERO deviates from ADR-0047, which added it deliberately ("a welcoming onboarding band, not a metric") — golden rule 7 means that is an ADR, not a delete. The user is right on the merits: the onboarding value belongs in the conditional Get-started card (item 2), which by construction shows exactly when onboarding is what the user needs, and nowhere else. SCOPE SPLIT 2026-07-17 (mid-implementation, surfaced to the user): the activity chart (user item 5) moved to F-084. Reason found while designing it, not guessed: aggregating the audit trail needs a store-level GROUP BY, because the alternative — the API paging the whole window into memory to count it — is the same thing this feature refused to let the CLIENT do, one layer down. That means an AuditLog PORT change (both adapters + the conformance suite, E-020) and it collides with the known F-078 debt; ADR-0051 also records that both adapters hardcode descending seq, so there is not even a cheap "oldest event" query to anchor the honest window on. That is a feature, not a rider on a UI change — folding it in here would have produced exactly the one enormous unreviewable commit the delivery split exists to avoid (golden rule 2/6). ADR-0053 clause 3 already records the chart DECISION; F-084 implements it. DONE 2026-07-17: HeroBand deleted (sr-only h1 keeps the route announceable — the hero owned the only h1); GetStarted gated on /v1/stats with unknown-state showing NO onboarding; feed bounded at max-h-[22rem] with tabIndex on the ul. ADR-0053 written; ADR-0047 marked superseded-in-part; DESIGN-SYSTEM §11 budget + mascot placements edited to match (E-004: the budget is what design-review audits against, so the doc could not lag). Gates green (state/typecheck 38-38/lint 22-22/format/test 36-36/build 19-19) + home.spec.ts 6/6 INCLUDING the axe WCAG A/AA sweep, so the tabIndex is proven not asserted. Screenshots captured for both states. Found in the gates: jsx-a11y/no-noninteractive-tabindex vs axe scrollable-region-focusable genuinely conflict; resolved in eslint config (repo convention) narrowed to the ul tag — roles:[list] fails (implicit role unresolved) and explicit role=list trips no-redundant-roles.

features.title: Source scans block the request: make them async jobs with real progress and honest state
acceptance: POST /v1/sources/:id/scan returns promptly (202 + the scan state) instead of awaiting the whole pipeline; the scan runs as a tracked background job

Scan progress is observable — processed/total over SSE — so the UI shows a determinate progressbar rather than an unbounded spinner (there is no progress DATA today, which is why item 11 is not a UI-only fix)
scanStatus reports queued|running(with progress)|idle|error, and a second scan of a source already scanning is rejected cleanly rather than racing
The dashboard handles every scan state honestly: queued, running with progress, completed, failed with the error
A background scan failure is surfaced (status + SSE), never swallowed — the request that started it is long gone
Scans stay incremental + idempotent; existing ingestion tests and the Local drain semantics stay green
api + ingestion + web gates green
features.notes: REPORTED BY THE USER (2026-07-17), items 11/12/13/14. CONFIRMED IN CODE: packages/ingestion/src/sources/service.ts performScan() awaits coordinator.scan() AND `await queue.drain?.()` — the F-038 doc comment says this out loud ("so a Local scan is synchronous-complete"). It was a deliberate, defensible choice for a Local profile (the summary reflects fully-processed work); it stops being defensible once the same process serves an SSE stream and a dashboard, because the drain is CPU-bound embedding and it owns the event loop for the duration. Hence items 11 (no expected time — there is no progress DATA to show, not just no progressbar) and 12 (blocks the API) are ONE defect with two faces. SCOPE DECISION (user, 2026-07-17, via AskUserQuestion): worker_threads + async jobs ONLY. Multi-PROCESS clustering was explicitly considered and DEFERRED, because it would break more than it fixes today: the event bus (packages/config/src/profiles/local.ts:265 createEventBus) and the scan-status map (sources/service.ts:96 new Map()) are BOTH in-process, so an SSE client connected to worker A would never see events emitted by worker B — reintroducing F-079 at the infrastructure layer, where it is far harder to see. Clustering needs a shared bus + externalized scan state (Redis pub/sub, BullMQ), which is precisely F-056 scope; chain it there rather than half-doing it here. Node already serves parallel I/O concurrently — threads are the answer to the CPU-bound half, and only to that half. Honest naming matters here: this makes scans NON-BLOCKING and PARALLEL-CAPABLE within one process; it does not make the API multi-process, and nothing should claim it does. SCOPE SPLIT 2026-07-17: the worker-thread pool (user item 13) moved to F-085, because it is a separate subsystem (a new Embeddings adapter) and this feature already spans ingestion + API + web. MEASURED FIRST, and the measurement REVERSED the initial reading — see F-085 notes for the numbers. The async job is the fix for what the user actually reported (the request hangs with no expected time); the pool is the fix for the collateral damage (every OTHER request eats a ~36ms stall per embedded chunk while a scan runs). Landing them separately keeps each verifiable.

features.title: Dashboard surface fixes: graph chrome + panel overflow, search clipping, settings, signin
acceptance: Graph: MiniMap and Controls removed; the React Flow attribution is RETAINED (hideAttribution stays false) but restyled as a discreet muted credit, legible on hover and never removed from the DOM
Graph: the node panel is bounded to the canvas height and scrolls internally — a node with many effects must not grow the page — and the scroll region is keyboard-reachable
Search: result cards are no longer clipped on the left; the active row ring renders in full on both edges
Settings: section-header icons removed from every card; Governance stops leaking an internal requirement id at users and gains the description every other card has
Signin: the "Context & Memory OS" badge is removed from the left pane
apps/web gates green; axe WCAG A/AA clean on the touched routes; screenshot evidence
features.notes: REPORTED BY THE USER (2026-07-17), items 6/7/8/9/10/16. ATTRIBUTION DECISION (user, 2026-07-17, via AskUserQuestion): KEEP IT, restyle it. This was surfaced rather than silently flipped because proOptions={{ hideAttribution: false }} in graph-canvas-impl.tsx is deliberate, not an oversight, and the question is ethical rather than technical: @xyflow/react is MIT, so hiding the mark is LEGALLY permitted, but xyflow explicitly asks that you subscribe to React Flow Pro if you do. The repo already has a NOTICE.md attribution culture (ADR-0013/0021/0038) and the user chose to stay consistent with it — the mark stays, it just stops shouting. MiniMap/Controls are pure UI chrome and go. Item 7 (side panel overflow) is confirmed: graph-side-panel.tsx has no overflow/max-height constraint at all, so a high-degree node simply grows the document. Item 8 ("border being cut off" on the left) is almost certainly a clipped focus-ring/shadow on an overflow container rather than the cards themselves — diagnose before restyling; the fix is on the scroll parent. Items 9/10 are judgment work against DESIGN-SYSTEM.md, not bug fixes: the user singled out Appearance as the section that already meets the bar, so it is the in-repo reference to match, not a section to touch. Note F-064 (Dashboard: UX-baseline completion + i18n readiness) is adjacent — keep this feature to the reported surfaces and do not absorb it (golden rule 2). SCOPE SPLIT 2026-07-17: the inspector rebuild (user item 9) moved to F-086. Reason: it is ~1022 lines across six components and was ALREADY rebuilt three commits ago by F-062 ("Inspector v2") — the user looking at that result and still calling it unprofessional means the gap is design judgment, not a missing pass, and a rushed edit tacked onto a batch of one-line fixes would be F-062 again. It needs its own plan, its own screenshot iteration, and the design-review skill. The other five items here are diagnosed defects with mechanical fixes and land now. DONE 2026-07-17: MiniMap/Controls removed; attribution KEPT and restyled to a muted 9px 0.45-opacity credit (legible on hover, never display:none) — and the first attempt SILENTLY HALF-FAILED: @xyflow/react/dist/style.css is imported by the component so it lands after globals.css, and at equal specificity the library wins every property IT declares (font-size/opacity applied, background/color did not, leaving the grey badge). A screenshot caught it; fixed by prefixing .react-flow; verified by computed style (rgba(150,150,150,0.25)/#999 -> transparent/#a1a1a1). Panel bounded lg:max-h-[65vh] + role=region + tabIndex (Effects mode contains nothing focusable); MEASURED: 25-effect hub node -> 0px page overflow. Search pr-1 -> px-1 (the container clipped the active rows 2px ring on the left; overflow-y:auto forces x-axis clipping). Settings icons removed on all 4 cards; Governance prose -> dl, dropping the leaked NFR-13. Gates green (typecheck 40-40/lint 23-23/format/test 38-38/build 20-20) + e2e 12/12 with axe AA across graph/search/settings/auth + screenshots.

features.title: The dashboard still ships brand mark v1: adopt mark v2 everywhere via a shared brand package
acceptance: Mark v2 (BRAND.md §4) is the only Tessera mark rendered anywhere in the dashboard — sidebar, header, signin, favicon/metadata, and any loading or error surface

The mark lives in ONE shared package consumed by both apps/web and apps/marketing, so the two cannot silently diverge again — the divergence this feature exists to fix is the proof that duplication does not hold
The ember gradient resolves correctly against all 4 dashboard themes x light/dark (the mark carries --rose/--gold; the dashboard token set must actually define them)
Multiple marks on one page do not collide on the gradient id (the marketing implementation already anticipates this via emberId — preserve it)
web + marketing gates green; contrast gate passes; screenshot evidence per the frontend quality bar
features.notes: REPORTED BY THE USER (2026-07-17), item 15 — and it is a genuine cross-app divergence, not a preference. apps/marketing/components/logo.tsx carries "Tessera mark v2 (BRAND.md §4, masters in docs/design/brand/)": a 3x3 ivory mosaic with the top-right tile lifted out and gilded in the ember gradient, plus a lowercase serif wordmark ("never bold"). apps/web/components/logo.tsx still carries v1: a monochrome pixel mark ("scattered tesserae converge into a solid core") with a sans wordmark. Same brand, two marks, and BRAND.md is authoritative — so the dashboard is simply WRONG, and has been since v2 landed on marketing. ROOT CAUSE IS DUPLICATION, and fixing only the pixels would leave it: two hand-maintained copies of a brand asset in two apps drift the moment one is updated, which is exactly what happened. Extract to a shared package instead — @tessera/mascot is the in-repo precedent for a shared brand asset consumed by both apps, so this follows a proven pattern rather than inventing one. WATCH THE TOKENS: v2 references var(--rose)/var(--gold) and font-serif, which exist in the marketing token set; the dashboard runs the F-070 4-theme system and may not define them. Verify before porting — a mark whose gradient silently resolves to transparent in 3 of 8 theme combinations is worse than the v1 it replaced. The "Context & Memory OS" badge removals (F-080 hero, F-082 signin) are related brand-surface cleanup but tracked there, not here. DONE 2026-07-17: @tessera/brand created (mirrors @tessera/mascot); both apps consume it; apps/web/components/logo.tsx + apps/marketing/components/logo.tsx DELETED so the duplication that caused the drift is gone. Closed --brand-* contract falling back to currentColor => monochrome (BRAND.md-sanctioned), so the missing --rose/--gold in web can never render an invisible ember. Mark does NOT track theme (BRAND.md forbids recoloring the mark; ADR-0047 licensed that for the MASCOT only) — per-MODE only. Instrument Serif added as --font-brand, deliberately not a theme face. Also fixed beyond the report: sidebar hand-rolled bold-sans "Tessera" -> lockup lowercase serif; dashboard had NO favicon -> app/icon.tsx. Gates green (typecheck 40-40/lint 23-23/format/test 38-38/build 20-20) + marketing 48 e2e green (the regression surface, since it already rendered v2) + screenshots across themes x modes. Accepted exception: icon.tsx restates geometry — Satori cannot rasterize SVG gradients, so it cannot consume the shared component (marketing already had this).

features.title: Overview activity chart: a store-level audit aggregation that cannot render a pruned day as silence
acceptance: GET /v1/stats/activity returns daily buckets by action group for one tenant, aggregated AT THE STORE (SQL GROUP BY) — never by paging the window into the API to count it
The response reports the `from` the server ACTUALLY used, and the chart labels that window rather than the requested one — ADR-0053 clause 3

The series never starts earlier than the oldest event the trail holds, derived from the DATA (not from config.audit.retention), so it is correct under maxAgeDays pruning, under maxEntries pruning, and for a workspace that is three days old
A pruned or absent day is distinguishable from a quiet one — a zero must never mean "we deleted the record"

The aggregation is a first-class AuditLog port method covered by the SHARED conformance suite, so both adapters are proven to agree (do not repeat the F-078 divergence: a contract only the reference adapter honours is not a contract)

The chart renders below the stat cards and only when there is data; it uses the existing ui/chart.tsx (shadcn/Recharts, DESIGN-SYSTEM §0) and --chart-* tokens so it is theme-true across 4 themes x light/dark
Reads (*.read) are excluded — the series covers work (ingestion/sources, memory writes, compiles, searches), not page views
No trend/delta field is added to /v1/stats — that refusal stands, for the reason the schema gives
api + web gates green; contrast gate passes
features.notes: SPLIT OUT OF F-080 (2026-07-17) — user item 5, "show some activity graph wrt time, only when we have data". The decision is already recorded in ADR-0053 clause 3; this implements it. WHY IT IS ITS OWN FEATURE: the honest, scalable version needs an AuditLog PORT method (aggregate at the store), which ripples to both adapters + the conformance suite (E-020) and lands squarely in F-078 territory. THE TRAP, and the reason this cannot be built naively: apps/api/src/schemas/stats.ts already refuses trend fields IN ITS OWN WORDS because "deriving one from createdAt would silently lie in any deployment using retention (FR-15), which deletes" — and the audit trail IS pruned (AuditLog.prune + config.audit.retention, since F-027). So a naive "last 30 days" histogram draws zeros for pruned days, and a zero meaning "we deleted the record" is indistinguishable from one meaning "nothing happened". ADR-0053 closes this by anchoring the window on the oldest event the trail actually HOLDS, derived from the data rather than from config — which is the only form that survives maxEntries pruning (no time-based clamp can see it) and is simpler besides. NOTE ADR-0051: both adapters hardcode descending seq and AuditQuery has no sort param, so "oldest retained event" is NOT a cheap query today — resolve it in the port aggregation rather than by paging to the end of the trail. NOT FR-47/F-057: that is per-tenant metering for billing; this is a histogram of actions the trail already records. The audit log is the ONLY real persisted time series available — the graph has no per-node createdAt and the ingestion manifest holds only path -> contentHash (both stated in stats.ts). ui/chart.tsx (shadcn/Recharts) is already installed and has ZERO consumers; this would be its first, so expect to prove the token wiring rather than assume it.

features.title: Embedding holds the event loop: move it to a bounded worker-thread pool (measured, not assumed)
acceptance: A worker_threads-pool Embeddings adapter implements the SAME Embeddings port (embed/embedBatch/info) — swappable per ADR-0006, with no caller change
The pool is bounded and configurable, and defaults sanely: each worker loads its OWN model copy (~3s init, ~90MB resident measured), so the default must not be cpus-1 by reflex

Measured proof that it works: p99 event-loop delay during a scan drops toward the offloaded control, using the same calibrated harness that produced the original finding (a claim of "now non-blocking" without a re-measurement is exactly what this feature exists to disprove)
Degrades to the in-process adapter when workers are unavailable, and tears down cleanly on shutdown (no leaked threads, no orphaned model memory)

Model load happens off the request path (it is a ~3s on-thread block today, absorbed at composition because createTransformersEmbeddings awaits pipeline() at construction — verify it stays that way, or moves to the workers)
ai + api gates green
features.notes: SPLIT OUT OF F-081 (2026-07-17) — user item 13 ("use multi-threading, handle parallel requests"). THE USER WAS RIGHT, AND MY FIRST MEASUREMENT SAID OTHERWISE — recorded because the near-miss is the lesson. @huggingface/transformers runs ONNX via onnxruntime-node, whose async run() is widely assumed to offload to the libuv threadpool. Take 1 measured MAX event-loop lag (68.7ms) and concluded "not blocking" — WRONG: max lag cannot separate "inference offloads, the lag is JS tokenization" from "inference runs on-thread in 36ms chunks", because both peak the same. Take 2 (cumulative lag) and take 3 (monitorEventLoopDelay) both read a KNOWN busy-loop control as 0% blocked — while the thread is held, no sample is ever taken, so the instrument goes blind exactly when the answer matters. The control caught the instrument three times; that is what controls are for. Take 4 calibrated against BOTH hypotheses in the real SHAPE of the work (24 x 36ms with await boundaries): mean loop delay — offloaded control 16.7ms, EMBEDDING 32.9ms, on-thread control 36.1ms. Embedding reads as ON-THREAD: onnxruntime-node does NOT give us a free thread here, and every concurrent request eats a ~36ms stall per embedded chunk for the whole scan. So the pool is warranted on evidence. WATCH THE COST, it is real and cuts against a big default: a worker cannot share the loaded model, so N workers = N model loads (~3s each, measured) and N copies resident (~90MB each). Multi-PROCESS clustering remains OUT (F-081 notes + ADR: in-process bus + scan-status map; F-056 is the prerequisite). Re-run the calibrated harness to prove the fix — do not assert it.

features.title: Inspector v3: the context package needs to read like an instrument, not a stack of cards
acceptance: The inspector reads as one instrument with a clear primary read, not four sibling cards of equal weight competing for attention
The compile trace and package scores are legible at a glance — a user can answer "what did the compiler pick, and why" without reading every card
Judged against DESIGN-SYSTEM.md via the design-review skill, with before/after screenshots across 4 themes x light/dark
No fabricated data; empty/error/loading states stay honest (F-062 got this right — do not regress it)
apps/web gates green; axe WCAG A/AA clean; contrast gate passes
features.notes: SPLIT OUT OF F-082 (2026-07-17) — user item 9: "The UI/UX of the inspector page is not professional. Make it professional and enterprise grade, ready for production." THE IMPORTANT CONTEXT: F-062 rebuilt this page as "Inspector v2" only three commits before the report (1469b6e), and the user is looking at THAT and still calling it unprofessional. So this is not a missing pass — another quick pass would produce F-062 again. Treat the ask as a design problem: 1022 lines across inspector-view/compile-form/fragment-card/package-export/package-guidance/recent-compiles, currently a vertical stack of same-weight `bg-sidebar` cards (scores, sections, trace) with no visual hierarchy, so nothing leads and the eye has no entry point. Start from what the page is FOR — answering "what context did the compiler select, and why" — and let the layout say that, rather than restyling the existing cards. Use the design-review + frontend-craft skills, and screenshot BEFORE deciding (the F-061 lesson: a screenshot caught a dark-theme ring bug no test could see). Do NOT regress F-062 honest empty guidance or the agent-ready export — those were the point of v2 and they work.
